title: Who needs an rdb?
content_html:
Matt Doherty

kdb+ is famous for its dislike of loops (no stinking loops).  As kdb+ developers we have a lot of flexibility in the architectures we can use, and today I’m going to try to convince you there’s something else we should remove from our systems: rdbs.  No stinking rdbs!

In kdb+ land there are essentially two ways to store data: in-memory, and on-disk.  The data itself is identical.  This was a key insight that’s now quite common in the columnar data space (see Apache Arrow in particular), as it removes the need for serialization when moving data from one place to another, and enables memory mapping and very fast reads of data from disk.  kdb+ supports a range of different architectures, but the standard or default model is still to use two main types of process together: real-time databases (rdbs) with the data fully in the process’s own memory space, and historical databases (hdbs) with the data serialized on disk and memory mapped into the processes memory space.

My opening is intentionally provocative and a little tongue in cheek.  I don’t really believe we shouldn’t use rdbs at all.  A major strength of kdb+ as a technology is its flexibility, and kdb-x promises to add even more.  The argument I’ll make here is that rdbs should not be considered the default option in all kdb systems. They have always been one of the core process types in kdb, but I believe for many problems they may not be required, and you should at least consider not using them. The approach isn’t new, and is deployed in a number of installations and in some of KX’s own components in their Insights product, but the details around when and where it might be applicable isn’t always clear.

Why have an rdb?

Faster queries

Queries against data in memory are of course much faster, aren’t they?  I put together a simple benchmark on fairly standard hardware with fairly standard data:

  • a i7i.xlarge ec2 instance with 32GB of memory and fast NVMe SSD storage attached
  • a single typical (relatively small) day of NYSE TAQ trade data.  Roughly 50 million trades and 10GB on disk
  • Comparing the performance of queries against in-memory data (rdb), on-disk data (hdb), and on-disk data when the cache is cold (hdb cold cache)
  • With a small set of queries covering some typical workloads

So the key piece of context before we talk more about these results, is that hdb processes often won’t actually to go all the way to disk to get their data.  They memory map files on disk, and when accessed the OS will first check the page cache, and if a particular file exists in that cache it will not actually go to disk to get it.  So in all of the hdb results above the query is hitting the page cache instead of disk i.e. the cache is warm.  So when the cache is warm the hdb isn’t so different from the rdb: it’s data is in memory, but in the OS level page cache instead of the processes own memory space.  I’ve also added a 3rd set of benchmarks when the cache is “cold”, so we can see the difference when the OS has to actually go to disk.

So what are the key takeaways from these benchmarks:

  • Let’s not bury the lede here: in-memory is the fastest for all benchmarks
  • If a query can use attributes and the result size is small in-memory data wins by a large margin over disk.  The last query in particular is vastly faster when the data is in memory and has attributes.  These queries are effectively lookups on a hash map, and so O(1) and very fast.  We can add attributes to data on disk too, but on disk attributes are not appendable in kdb+ so we can’t maintain them.  I’ll come back to this point later.
  • If the query does not use attributes, or it does but the result size is larger, the performance gap between the rdb and hdb is small.  Depending on the query it hovers between 0-20%.  This small difference likely comes from minor page faults and TLB pressure and other technical stuff when accessing the page cache that it’s not worth worrying about too much.  The data in memory in the OS page cache is not quite as fast as data in the rdbs heap, but it’s close.  The aggregation and large scan (with attributes on filter) comparisons above are maybe the most interesting: both of these queries benefit from attributes, but the hdb without attributes is still not far behind, as the query still has to access a lot of data so the attribute benefit is much less.  So really an hdb can be thought of as another type of in-memory database, with the memory in a shared space: the OS page cache
  • Even when a query has to go all the way to disk, the difference in speed is still not enormous.  We’re talking 2x at worst.  This is where modern NVMe SSDs make a massive difference.  On spinning disk this difference would be much much larger.

Keep up with inserts

As well as reading data, we also have to be able to add new data.  New trades flow in during the day and we need to be able to add them.  Here is another benchmark comparing the two setups:

The in-memory process has a more clear-cut advantage here.  But it’s perhaps not as large as you might expect.  It’s certainly much smaller than when your storage was slow spinning disks.  And another crucial point here: inserts into memory are a serial process.  When an rdb is inserting new data it cannot serve queries simultaneously.  This is not true in the later case, we can insert to a splayed table on disk and at the same time processes can serve queries on the data.  Compute and memory are separated.

Why not have an rdb?

Faster queries

I know above I showed that the rdb is (ever so slightly) faster, but that’s for one query. What if you have 2, or 10? On an rdb you’re stuck running queries serially.  It cannot run queries concurrently.  However if your data is shared on disk (and in memory via the OS page cache) you can go parallel.  Each individual query might take slightly longer, but you can run all 10 at once on separate processes.  A query that is 10% slower on an hdb, becomes 45% faster if you need to run it twice as you can go parallel e.g. two queries at 110ms each running in parallel taking 110ms total vs two queries at 100ms serially taking 200ms.  This is also a big architectural advantage if you want to support different types of use-case on the same system.  For example research queries, and more latency sensitive trading queries, without them getting in each others way.

All memory is shared by default

We can shard and scale basically for free.  rdbs have a fixed memory cost, if we want more processes we need more memory, and new processes are slow to startup as they need to read all current data into memory.  When your data is only stored once, on disk and in the OS page cache, you can have 10 processes on top of it, or why not 100?  These processes can startup almost instantly.  This fundamentally separates memory and compute

No gateways

You can of course still have gateway processes if you want, but you don’t need them to join rdb and hdb data together.  It’s possible to have all your data accessible in one process.  Users can spin up their own single process with access to all data, both live and historical.

Simpler architecture

There are some details to consider on reloading and mapping data on disk when it’s being appended to “live”, but on the whole you have fewer processes, and fewer process types. There’s no coordination problem between rdb and hdbs and gateways, so your system may well be simpler.

So let’s talk a little more about this…

Alternative default architecture

Standard kdb+ architecture is the way it is for a good reason.  If you’re in a world where memory is much more expensive, and all of your non-volatile storage is spinning disks, it makes a lot more sense to split your database in two.  Inserts against splayed tables on disk will be much slower than memory, likely several orders of magnitude, and any queries that do not hit the OS page cache (cold cache) will be drastically slower, again probably several orders of magnitude.  But this is no longer the world we live in.

I’m proposing an alternate default architecture that looks more like this:

The tickerplant process is exactly the same as before, but all data goes straight to disk via “writer” processes (essentially TorQ wdb processes), and all data is read via database processes which mount the data on disk (TorQ hdbs).

Let me be clear here that this isn’t a revolutionary idea by any means.  I’m sure there are kdb+ systems in operation today that have a similar architecture to this.  My argument here is less that this is new, but rather that we should consider this alternative much more seriously and broadly.

Here’s a clearer picture showing how we have on-disk data separated into a “live partition” and all your standard historical data:

I believe this approach gives us a few significant advantages:

  • It is simpler.  Fewer processes fewer problems
  • More flexible and scalable.  Compute and memory are separated.  If we need more database processes we can simply add them, they do not have direct memory overhead and they will spin up very quickly (unlike traditional rdbs).  We’re effectively using the OS page cache as “free” memory management, and this is core battle tested OS code so it’s very solid.
  • The data is in one place.  If you need today and previous days data there’s no need to run two queries against two different processes and handle joining etc.  All data is in one place

Of course everything in software engineering is trade-offs, and here the trade-offs we’re making are:

  • Somewhat slower query performance compared to an rdb.  As covered above the difference is a lot less than you might think, but there is a real difference.
  • In the partition that is being live appended to we cannot have attributes.  This means for certain “small lookup” type queries the performance difference is dramatic.  However these queries may not be an important part of your workflow, or if you want the advantages above while keeping these types of queries fast, you can add dedicated cache processes for what you need i.e. add specialized caches based on need, rather than a large expensive general purpose cache process (i.e. the rdb)

There are also a few key technical considerations for “live append” databases (dbs, no need to call them hdb and rdb anymore):

  • We want to lock the “live partition” into the OS page cache to make sure queries rarely/never have to go to disk.  This will happen naturally if the data is used a lot as the OS will try to keep frequently accessed data in the cache.  However, we can also use vmtouch or tmpfs (RAMdisk) to directly control this.  So we can guarantee we always get the hot cache benchmark results above and never have to go to disk.  This is relatively simple to setup.
  • There is a coordination problem between the database processes and the symfiles (enumerations) and columns being live appended. This is not quite as bad as it seems.  I believe the best way to solve this to use .Q.MAP to avoid mmap overhead on every query, and \l to reload the symfile and remap the latest data periodically.  In principle this can be very fast (in my test setup above running \l takes 0.018ms), and we have a few options on how exactly to execute depending on the trade-offs between availability and speed we want to make in our system:
    • if it’s cheap enough you can simply run this before any query
    • or you can have the writer trigger a reload on all dbs on every insert so we only reload when there is new data
    • or it can be timer based
  • the symfile has to be kept small as we’ll be reloading it a lot.  Although we can be smart about this, and reload it optimistically i.e. use the chksum to quickly see if it has changed, and only reload if required.
  • we cannot have attributes in the “live” partition.  Traditionally hdb partitions put a p attribute on the most commonly filtered column, which acts like an index for lookups.  Attributes on disk in kdb+ are fundamentally not appendable, so we have to do without them.  However we can still sort and add them as normal at rollover as data moves to history and out of the “live partition”

Based on the requirements of your specific system the architecture above can then be expanded with realtime engines and/or cache processes to calculate analytics (for example VWAPs, bars etc.).  These values can then be published back into the tickerplant and read from the database processes, or accessed directly

I don’t want to dwell too much on these technical details, so I’ll aim to follow up with details on how to setup this kind of architecture with TorQ:

  • How to reload and remap data as quickly as possible
  • Rollover
  • Making it as easy as possible to setup and get going

I’m not one for long conclusions, but hopefully I’ve made you at least consider an alternate default architecture for kdb+. “no rdb” architectures can be simpler, more scalable and nearly as fast.  I’m certainly not saying this is the “correct” way, simply that it’s something we should consider a little more strongly.  If anyone else has thoughts or wants to discuss these ideas further we’re always happy to hear from you!

sentences.en: a single typical (relatively small) day of NYSE TAQ trade data.
sentences.zh: 纽约证券交易所 TAQ 交易数据的单个典型（相对较小）日。

sentences.en: Roughly 50 million trades and 10GB on disk
sentences.zh: 大约 5000 万笔交易和 10GB 磁盘空间

blocks.html_en: • Comparing the performance of queries against in-memory data (rdb), on-disk data (hdb), and on-disk data when the cache is cold (hdb cold cache)

sentences.en: Comparing the performance of queries against in-memory data (rdb), on-disk data (hdb), and on-disk data when the cache is cold (hdb cold cache)
sentences.zh: 比较内存数据（rdb）、磁盘数据（hdb）和缓存冷时磁盘数据（hdb 冷缓存）的查询性能

blocks.html_en: • With a small set of queries covering some typical workloads

sentences.en: With a small set of queries covering some typical workloads
sentences.zh: 通过一小组涵盖一些典型工作负载的查询

blocks.html_en: So the key piece of context before we talk more about these results, is that hdb processes often won’t actually to go all the way to disk to get their data.  They memory map files on disk, and when accessed the OS will first check the page cache, and if a particular file exists in that cache it will not actually go to disk to get it.  So in all of the hdb results above the query is hitting the page cache instead of disk i.e. the cache is warm.  So when the cache is warm the hdb isn’t so different from the rdb: it’s data is in memory, but in the OS level page cache instead of the processes own memory space.  I’ve also added a 3rd set of benchmarks when the cache is “cold”, so we can see the difference when the OS has to actually go to disk.

sentences.en: So the key piece of context before we talk more about these results, is that hdb processes often won’t actually to go all the way to disk to get their data.
sentences.zh: 因此，在我们更多地讨论这些结果之前，关键的背景是 hdb 进程实际上通常不会一直到磁盘来获取数据。

sentences.en: They memory map files on disk, and when accessed the OS will first check the page cache, and if a particular file exists in that cache it will not actually go to disk to get it.
sentences.zh: 它们将文件内存映射到磁盘上，当访问时，操作系统将首先检查页面缓存，如果该缓存中存在特定文件，它实际上不会去磁盘获取它。

sentences.en: So in all of the hdb results above the query is hitting the page cache instead of disk i.e. the cache is warm.
sentences.zh: 因此，在上面的所有 hdb 结果中，查询都会命中页面缓存而不是磁盘，即缓存是热的。

sentences.en: So when the cache is warm the hdb isn’t so different from the rdb: it’s data is in memory, but in the OS level page cache instead of the processes own memory space.
sentences.zh: 因此，当缓存处于热状态时，hdb 与 rdb 没有太大区别：它的数据位于内存中，但位于操作系统级页面缓存中，而不是进程自己的内存空间中。

sentences.en: I’ve also added a 3rd set of benchmarks when the cache is “cold”, so we can see the difference when the OS has to actually go to disk.
sentences.zh: 当缓存“冷”时，我还添加了第三组基准测试，因此我们可以看到操作系统必须实际访问磁盘时的差异。

blocks.html_en: So what are the key takeaways from these benchmarks:

sentences.en: So what are the key takeaways from these benchmarks:
sentences.zh: 那么这些基准测试的主要结论是什么：

blocks.html_en: • Let’s not bury the lede here: in-memory is the fastest for all benchmarks

sentences.en: Let’s not bury the lede here: in-memory is the fastest for all benchmarks
sentences.zh: 我们不要在这里埋葬lede：内存中是所有基准测试中最快的

blocks.html_en: • If a query can use attributes and the result size is small in-memory data wins by a large margin over disk.  The last query in particular is vastly faster when the data is in memory and has attributes.  These queries are effectively lookups on a hash map, and so O(1) and very fast.  We can add attributes to data on disk too, but on disk attributes are not appendable in kdb+ so we can’t maintain them.  I’ll come back to this point later.

sentences.en: If a query can use attributes and the result size is small in-memory data wins by a large margin over disk.
sentences.zh: 如果查询可以使用属性并且结果大小较小，则内存中的数据将大大优于磁盘。

sentences.en: The last query in particular is vastly faster when the data is in memory and has attributes.
sentences.zh: 当数据位于内存中并且具有属性时，最后一个查询的速度尤其快。

sentences.en: These queries are effectively lookups on a hash map, and so O(1) and very fast.
sentences.zh: 这些查询实际上是对哈希图的查找，因此 O(1) 并且速度非常快。

sentences.en: We can add attributes to data on disk too, but on disk attributes are not appendable in kdb+ so we can’t maintain them.
sentences.zh: 我们也可以向磁盘上的数据添加属性，但磁盘上的属性不可在 kdb+ 中附加，因此我们无法维护它们。

sentences.en: I’ll come back to this point later.
sentences.zh: 我稍后会回到这一点。

blocks.html_en: • If the query does not use attributes, or it does but the result size is larger, the performance gap between the rdb and hdb is small.  Depending on the query it hovers between 0-20%.  This small difference likely comes from minor page faults and TLB pressure and other technical stuff when accessing the page cache that it’s not worth worrying about too much.  The data in memory in the OS page cache is not quite as fast as data in the rdbs heap, but it’s close.  The aggregation and large scan (with attributes on filter) comparisons above are maybe the most interesting: both of these queries benefit from attributes, but the hdb without attributes is still not far behind, as the query still has to access a lot of data so the attribute benefit is much less.  So really an hdb can be thought of as another type of in-memory database, with the memory in a shared space: the OS page cache

sentences.en: If the query does not use attributes, or it does but the result size is larger, the performance gap between the rdb and hdb is small.
sentences.zh: 如果查询不使用属性，或者使用属性但结果较大，则rdb和hdb之间的性能差距很小。

sentences.en: Depending on the query it hovers between 0-20%.
sentences.zh: 根据查询的不同，它徘徊在 0-20% 之间。

sentences.en: This small difference likely comes from minor page faults and TLB pressure and other technical stuff when accessing the page cache that it’s not worth worrying about too much.
sentences.zh: 这种微小的差异可能来自于访问页面缓存时的轻微页面错误和 TLB 压力以及其他技术问题，因此不值得过多担心。

sentences.en: The data in memory in the OS page cache is not quite as fast as data in the rdbs heap, but it’s close.
sentences.zh: 操作系统页面缓存中内存中的数据不如 RDBS 堆中的数据快，但也很接近。

sentences.en: The aggregation and large scan (with attributes on filter) comparisons above are maybe the most interesting: both of these queries benefit from attributes, but the hdb without attributes is still not far behind, as the query still has to access a lot of data so the attribute benefit is much less.
sentences.zh: 上面的聚合和大扫描（过滤器上有属性）的比较可能是最有趣的：这两个查询都受益于属性，但没有属性的 hdb 仍然不甘落后，因为查询仍然需要访问大量数据，因此属性的好处要少得多。

sentences.en: So really an hdb can be thought of as another type of in-memory database, with the memory in a shared space: the OS page cache
sentences.zh: 因此，实际上 hdb 可以被视为另一种类型的内存数据库，内存位于共享空间中：操作系统页面缓存

blocks.html_en: • Even when a query has to go all the way to disk, the difference in speed is still not enormous.  We’re talking 2x at worst.  This is where modern NVMe SSDs make a massive difference.  On spinning disk this difference would be much much larger.

sentences.en: Even when a query has to go all the way to disk, the difference in speed is still not enormous.
sentences.zh: 即使查询必须一直到达磁盘，速度差异仍然不是很大。

sentences.en: We’re talking 2x at worst.
sentences.zh: 我们说的是最坏情况下的两倍。

sentences.en: This is where modern NVMe SSDs make a massive difference.
sentences.zh: 这就是现代 NVMe SSD 发挥巨大作用的地方。

sentences.en: On spinning disk this difference would be much much larger.
sentences.zh: 在旋转磁盘上，这种差异会大得多。

sentences.en: Keep up with inserts
sentences.zh: 跟上插入内容

blocks.html_en: As well as reading data, we also have to be able to add new data.  New trades flow in during the day and we need to be able to add them.  Here is another benchmark comparing the two setups:

sentences.en: As well as reading data, we also have to be able to add new data.
sentences.zh: 除了读取数据之外，我们还必须能够添加新数据。

sentences.en: New trades flow in during the day and we need to be able to add them.
sentences.zh: 白天有新的交易流入，我们需要能够添加它们。

sentences.en: Here is another benchmark comparing the two setups:
sentences.zh: 这是比较这两种设置的另一个基准：

blocks.html_en: The in-memory process has a more clear-cut advantage here.  But it’s perhaps not as large as you might expect.  It’s certainly much smaller than when your storage was slow spinning disks.  And another crucial point here: inserts into memory are a serial process.  When an rdb is inserting new data it cannot serve queries simultaneously.  This is not true in the later case, we can insert to a splayed table on disk and at the same time processes can serve queries on the data.  Compute and memory are separated.

sentences.en: The in-memory process has a more clear-cut advantage here.
sentences.zh: 内存中进程在这里具有更明显的优势。

sentences.en: But it’s perhaps not as large as you might expect.
sentences.zh: 但它可能没有你想象的那么大。

sentences.en: It’s certainly much smaller than when your storage was slow spinning disks.
sentences.zh: 它肯定比您的存储是慢速旋转磁盘时要小得多。

sentences.en: And another crucial point here: inserts into memory are a serial process.
sentences.zh: 这里的另一个关键点：插入内存是一个串行过程。

sentences.en: When an rdb is inserting new data it cannot serve queries simultaneously.
sentences.zh: 当 RDB 插入新数据时，它无法同时提供查询服务。

sentences.en: This is not true in the later case, we can insert to a splayed table on disk and at the same time processes can serve queries on the data.
sentences.zh: 在后一种情况下情况并非如此，我们可以插入磁盘上的展开表，同时进程可以对数据进行查询。

sentences.en: Compute and memory are separated.
sentences.zh: 计算和内存是分开的。

blocks.html_en: Why not have an rdb?

sentences.en: Why not have an rdb?
sentences.zh: 为什么没有rdb？

blocks.html_en: Faster queries

sentences.en: Faster queries
sentences.zh: 更快的查询

blocks.html_en: I know above I showed that the rdb is (ever so slightly) faster, but that’s for one query. What if you have 2, or 10? On an rdb you’re stuck running queries serially.  It cannot run queries concurrently.  However if your data is shared on disk (and in memory via the OS page cache) you can go parallel.  Each individual query might take slightly longer, but you can run all 10 at once on separate processes.  A query that is 10% slower on an hdb, becomes 45% faster if you need to run it twice as you can go parallel e.g. two queries at 110ms each running in parallel taking 110ms total vs two queries at 100ms serially taking 200ms.  This is also a big architectural advantage if you want to support different types of use-case on the same system.  For example research queries, and more latency sensitive trading queries, without them getting in each others way.

sentences.en: I know above I showed that the rdb is (ever so slightly) faster, but that’s for one query.
sentences.zh: 我知道上面我展示了 RDB（稍微）更快，但这只是针对一个查询。

sentences.en: What if you have 2, or 10?
sentences.zh: 如果你有 2 个或 10 个怎么办？

sentences.en: On an rdb you’re stuck running queries serially.
sentences.zh: 在 RDB 上，您只能串行运行查询。

sentences.en: It cannot run queries concurrently.
sentences.zh: 它不能同时运行查询。

sentences.en: However if your data is shared on disk (and in memory via the OS page cache) you can go parallel.
sentences.zh: 但是，如果您的数据在磁盘上共享（并通过操作系统页面缓存在内存中），则可以并行。

sentences.en: Each individual query might take slightly longer, but you can run all 10 at once on separate processes.
sentences.zh: 每个单独的查询可能需要稍长的时间，但您可以在单独的进程上同时运行所有 10 个查询。

sentences.en: A query that is 10% slower on an hdb, becomes 45% faster if you need to run it twice as you can go parallel e.g. two queries at 110ms each running in parallel taking 110ms total vs two queries at 100ms serially taking 200ms.
sentences.zh: 在 hdb 上慢 10% 的查询，如果您需要运行两次，则速度会快 45%，因为您可以并行运行，例如两个以 110 毫秒的速度并行运行的查询总共花费 110 毫秒，而两个以 100 毫秒的速度串行运行的查询总共花费 200 毫秒。

sentences.en: This is also a big architectural advantage if you want to support different types of use-case on the same system.
sentences.zh: 如果您想在同一系统上支持不同类型的用例，这也是一个很大的架构优势。

sentences.en: For example research queries, and more latency sensitive trading queries, without them getting in each others way.
sentences.zh: 例如研究查询和对延迟更敏感的交易查询，而不会互相妨碍。

blocks.html_en: All memory is shared by default

sentences.en: All memory is shared by default
sentences.zh: 默认情况下所有内存都是共享的

blocks.html_en: We can shard and scale basically for free.  rdbs have a fixed memory cost, if we want more processes we need more memory, and new processes are slow to startup as they need to read all current data into memory.  When your data is only stored once, on disk and in the OS page cache, you can have 10 processes on top of it, or why not 100?  These processes can startup almost instantly.  This fundamentally separates memory and compute

sentences.en: We can shard and scale basically for free.
sentences.zh: 我们基本上可以免费进行分片和扩展。

sentences.en: rdbs have a fixed memory cost, if we want more processes we need more memory, and new processes are slow to startup as they need to read all current data into memory.
sentences.zh: RDB 具有固定的内存成本，如果我们想要更多进程，我们需要更多内存，而新进程启动速度很慢，因为它们需要将所有当前数据读入内存。

sentences.en: When your data is only stored once, on disk and in the OS page cache, you can have 10 processes on top of it, or why not 100?
sentences.zh: 当您的数据仅在磁盘和操作系统页面缓存中存储一​​次时，您可以在其上放置 10 个进程，或者为什么不可以放置 100 个呢？

sentences.en: These processes can startup almost instantly.
sentences.zh: 这些进程几乎可以立即启动。

sentences.en: This fundamentally separates memory and compute
sentences.zh: 这从根本上分离了内存和计算

blocks.html_en: No gateways

sentences.en: No gateways
sentences.zh: 无网关

blocks.html_en: You can of course still have gateway processes if you want, but you don’t need them to join rdb and hdb data together.  It’s possible to have all your data accessible in one process.  Users can spin up their own single process with access to all data, both live and historical.

sentences.en: You can of course still have gateway processes if you want, but you don’t need them to join rdb and hdb data together.
sentences.zh: 当然，如果您愿意，您仍然可以拥有网关进程，但您不需要它们将 rdb 和 hdb 数据连接在一起。

sentences.en: It’s possible to have all your data accessible in one process.
sentences.zh: 可以在一个进程中访问所有数据。

sentences.en: Users can spin up their own single process with access to all data, both live and historical.
sentences.zh: 用户可以启动自己的单一流程来访问所有数据，包括实时数据和历史数据。

blocks.html_en: Simpler architecture

sentences.en: Simpler architecture
sentences.zh: 更简单的架构

blocks.html_en: There are some details to consider on reloading and mapping data on disk when it’s being appended to “live”, but on the whole you have fewer processes, and fewer process types. There’s no coordination problem between rdb and hdbs and gateways, so your system may well be simpler.

sentences.en: There are some details to consider on reloading and mapping data on disk when it’s being appended to “live”, but on the whole you have fewer processes, and fewer process types.
sentences.zh: 当将数据附加到“实时”时，在磁盘上重新加载和映射数据时需要考虑一些细节，但总的来说，您的进程和进程类型都较少。

sentences.en: There’s no coordination problem between rdb and hdbs and gateways, so your system may well be simpler.
sentences.zh: rdb 和 hdb 以及网关之间不存在协调问题，因此您的系统可能会更简单。

blocks.html_en: So let’s talk a little more about this…

sentences.en: So let’s talk a little more about this…
sentences.zh: 那么让我们多谈谈这个……

blocks.html_en: Alternative default architecture

sentences.en: Alternative default architecture
sentences.zh: 替代默认架构

blocks.html_en: Standard kdb+ architecture is the way it is for a good reason.  If you’re in a world where memory is much more expensive, and all of your non-volatile storage is spinning disks, it makes a lot more sense to split your database in two.  Inserts against splayed tables on disk will be much slower than memory, likely several orders of magnitude, and any queries that do not hit the OS page cache (cold cache) will be drastically slower, again probably several orders of magnitude.  But this is no longer the world we live in.

sentences.en: Standard kdb+ architecture is the way it is for a good reason.
sentences.zh: 标准 kdb+ 架构之所以如此，是有充分理由的。

sentences.en: If you’re in a world where memory is much more expensive, and all of your non-volatile storage is spinning disks, it makes a lot more sense to split your database in two.
sentences.zh: 如果您所处的世界内存更加昂贵，并且所有非易失性存储都是旋转磁盘，那么将数据库一分为二更有意义。

sentences.en: Inserts against splayed tables on disk will be much slower than memory, likely several orders of magnitude, and any queries that do not hit the OS page cache (cold cache) will be drastically slower, again probably several orders of magnitude.
sentences.zh: 针对磁盘上展开表的插入将比内存慢得多，可能慢几个数量级，并且任何不命中操作系统页面缓存（冷缓存）的查询都会慢得多，同样可能慢几个数量级。

sentences.en: But this is no longer the world we live in.
sentences.zh: 但这不再是我们生活的世界。

blocks.html_en: I’m proposing an alternate default architecture that looks more like this:

sentences.en: I’m proposing an alternate default architecture that looks more like this:
sentences.zh: 我提出了一个替代的默认架构，看起来更像这样：

blocks.html_en: The tickerplant process is exactly the same as before, but all data goes straight to disk via “writer” processes (essentially TorQ wdb processes), and all data is read via database processes which mount the data on disk (TorQ hdbs).

sentences.en: The tickerplant process is exactly the same as before, but all data goes straight to disk via “writer” processes (essentially TorQ wdb processes), and all data is read via database processes which mount the data on disk (TorQ hdbs).
sentences.zh: tickerplant 进程与以前完全相同，但所有数据都通过“writer”进程（本质上是 TorQ wdb 进程）直接写入磁盘，并且所有数据都通过将数据装载到磁盘上的数据库进程（TorQ hdbs）读取。

blocks.html_en: Let me be clear here that this isn’t a revolutionary idea by any means.  I’m sure there are kdb+ systems in operation today that have a similar architecture to this.  My argument here is less that this is new, but rather that we should consider this alternative much more seriously and broadly.

sentences.en: Let me be clear here that this isn’t a revolutionary idea by any means.
sentences.zh: 让我在这里明确一点，这无论如何都不是一个革命性的想法。

sentences.en: I’m sure there are kdb+ systems in operation today that have a similar architecture to this.
sentences.zh: 我确信目前正在运行的 kdb+ 系统具有与此类似的架构。

sentences.en: My argument here is less that this is new, but rather that we should consider this alternative much more seriously and broadly.
sentences.zh: 我在这里的论点并不是说这是新的，而是我们应该更认真、更广泛地考虑这种替代方案。

blocks.html_en: Here’s a clearer picture showing how we have on-disk data separated into a “live partition” and all your standard historical data:

sentences.en: Here’s a clearer picture showing how we have on-disk data separated into a “live partition” and all your standard historical data:
sentences.zh: 这是一张更清晰的图片，显示了我们如何将磁盘数据分为“活动分区”和所有标准历史数据：

blocks.html_en: I believe this approach gives us a few significant advantages:

sentences.en: I believe this approach gives us a few significant advantages:
sentences.zh: 我相信这种方法给我们带来了一些显着的优势：

blocks.html_en: • It is simpler.  Fewer processes fewer problems

sentences.en: It is simpler.
sentences.zh: 更简单。

sentences.en: Fewer processes fewer problems
sentences.zh: 更少的流程更少的问题

blocks.html_en: • More flexible and scalable.  Compute and memory are separated.  If we need more database processes we can simply add them, they do not have direct memory overhead and they will spin up very quickly (unlike traditional rdbs).  We’re effectively using the OS page cache as “free” memory management, and this is core battle tested OS code so it’s very solid.

sentences.en: More flexible and scalable.
sentences.zh: 更加灵活和可扩展。

sentences.en: Compute and memory are separated.
sentences.zh: 计算和内存是分开的。

sentences.en: If we need more database processes we can simply add them, they do not have direct memory overhead and they will spin up very quickly (unlike traditional rdbs).
sentences.zh: 如果我们需要更多的数据库进程，我们可以简单地添加它们，它们没有直接的内存开销，并且它们会很快旋转（与传统的 RDB 不同）。

sentences.en: We’re effectively using the OS page cache as “free” memory management, and this is core battle tested OS code so it’s very solid.
sentences.zh: 我们有效地使用操作系统页面缓存作为“免费”内存管理，这是经过核心战斗测试的操作系统代码，因此非常可靠。

blocks.html_en: • The data is in one place.  If you need today and previous days data there’s no need to run two queries against two different processes and handle joining etc.  All data is in one place

sentences.en: The data is in one place.
sentences.zh: 数据位于一处。

sentences.en: If you need today and previous days data there’s no need to run two queries against two different processes and handle joining etc. All data is in one place
sentences.zh: 如果您需要今天和前几天的数据，则无需针对两个不同的进程运行两个查询并处理连接等。所有数据都位于一个位置

blocks.html_en: Of course everything in software engineering is trade-offs, and here the trade-offs we’re making are:

sentences.en: Of course everything in software engineering is trade-offs, and here the trade-offs we’re making are:
sentences.zh: 当然，软件工程中的一切都是权衡，这里我们所做的权衡是：

blocks.html_en: • Somewhat slower query performance compared to an rdb.  As covered above the difference is a lot less than you might think, but there is a real difference.

sentences.en: Somewhat slower query performance compared to an rdb.
sentences.zh: 与 RDB 相比，查询性能稍慢。

sentences.en: As covered above the difference is a lot less than you might think, but there is a real difference.
sentences.zh: 如上所述，差异比您想象的要小得多，但确实存在差异。

blocks.html_en: • In the partition that is being live appended to we cannot have attributes.  This means for certain “small lookup” type queries the performance difference is dramatic.  However these queries may not be an important part of your workflow, or if you want the advantages above while keeping these types of queries fast, you can add dedicated cache processes for what you need i.e. add specialized caches based on need, rather than a large expensive general purpose cache process (i.e. the rdb)

sentences.en: In the partition that is being live appended to we cannot have attributes.
sentences.zh: 在实时附加的分区中，我们不能有属性。

sentences.en: This means for certain “small lookup” type queries the performance difference is dramatic.
sentences.zh: 这意味着对于某些“小型查找”类型的查询，性能差异是巨大的。

sentences.en: However these queries may not be an important part of your workflow, or if you want the advantages above while keeping these types of queries fast, you can add dedicated cache processes for what you need i.e. add specialized caches based on need, rather than a large expensive general purpose cache process (i.e. the rdb)
sentences.zh: 然而，这些查询可能不是工作流程的重要部分，或者如果您希望在保持这些类型的查询快速的同时获得上述优势，您可以根据需要添加专用缓存进程，即根据需要添加专用缓存，而不是大型昂贵的通用缓存进程（即rdb）

blocks.html_en: There are also a few key technical considerations for “live append” databases (dbs, no need to call them hdb and rdb anymore):

sentences.en: There are also a few key technical considerations for “live append” databases (dbs, no need to call them hdb and rdb anymore):
sentences.zh: 对于“实时追加”数据库（db，不再需要称它们为 hdb 和 rdb）还有一些关键的技术考虑因素：

blocks.html_en: • We want to lock the “live partition” into the OS page cache to make sure queries rarely/never have to go to disk.  This will happen naturally if the data is used a lot as the OS will try to keep frequently accessed data in the cache.  However, we can also use vmtouch or tmpfs (RAMdisk) to directly control this.  So we can guarantee we always get the hot cache benchmark results above and never have to go to disk.  This is relatively simple to setup.

sentences.en: We want to lock the “live partition” into the OS page cache to make sure queries rarely/never have to go to disk.
sentences.zh: 我们希望将“活动分区”锁定到操作系统页面缓存中，以确保查询很少/永远不需要访问磁盘。

sentences.en: This will happen naturally if the data is used a lot as the OS will try to keep frequently accessed data in the cache.
sentences.zh: 如果数据被大量使用，这种情况就会自然发生，因为操作系统会尝试将频繁访问的数据保留在缓存中。

sentences.en: However, we can also use vmtouch or tmpfs (RAMdisk) to directly control this.
sentences.zh: 不过，我们也可以使用 vmtouch 或 tmpfs (RAMdisk) 来直接控制它。

sentences.en: So we can guarantee we always get the hot cache benchmark results above and never have to go to disk.
sentences.zh: 因此，我们可以保证我们始终获得上面的热缓存基准测试结果，而无需访问磁盘。

sentences.en: This is relatively simple to setup.
sentences.zh: 这设置起来相对简单。

blocks.html_en:
• There is a coordination problem between the database processes and the symfiles (enumerations) and columns being live appended. This is not quite as bad as it seems.  I believe the best way to solve this to use .Q.MAP to avoid mmap overhead on every query, and \l to reload the symfile and remap the latest data periodically.  In principle this can be very fast (in my test setup above running \l takes 0.018ms), and we have a few options on how exactly to execute depending on the trade-offs between availability and speed we want to make in our system:
    • if it’s cheap enough you can simply run this before any query
    • or you can have the writer trigger a reload on all dbs on every insert so we only reload when there is new data
    • or it can be timer based

sentences.en: There is a coordination problem between the database processes and the symfiles (enumerations) and columns being live appended.
sentences.zh: 数据库进程和符号文件（枚举）以及实时附加的列之间存在协调问题。

sentences.en: This is not quite as bad as it seems.
sentences.zh: 这并不像看起来那么糟糕。

sentences.en: I believe the best way to solve this to use .Q.MAP to avoid mmap overhead on every query, and \l to reload the symfile and remap the latest data periodically.
sentences.zh: 我相信解决此问题的最佳方法是使用 .Q.MAP 来避免每个查询的 mmap 开销，并使用 \l 重新加载符号文件并定期重新映射最新数据。

sentences.en: In principle this can be very fast (in my test setup above running \l takes 0.018ms), and we have a few options on how exactly to execute depending on the trade-offs between availability and speed we want to make in our system: if it’s cheap enough you can simply run this before any query or you can have the writer trigger a reload on all dbs on every insert so we only reload when there is new data or it can be timer based
sentences.zh: 原则上，这可以非常快（在我上面的测试设置中，运行 \l 需要 0.018ms），并且我们有一些关于如何执行的选项，具体取决于我们想要在系统中进行的可用性和速度之间的权衡：如果它足够便宜，您可以简单地在任何查询之前运行它，或者您可以让编写器在每次插入时触发所有数据库的重新加载，这样我们只在有新数据时重新加载，或者它可以基于计时器

blocks.html_en: • if it’s cheap enough you can simply run this before any query

sentences.en: if it’s cheap enough you can simply run this before any query
sentences.zh: 如果它足够便宜，你可以在任何查询之前简单地运行它

blocks.html_en: • or you can have the writer trigger a reload on all dbs on every insert so we only reload when there is new data

sentences.en: or you can have the writer trigger a reload on all dbs on every insert so we only reload when there is new data
sentences.zh: 或者您可以让编写器在每次插入时触发所有数据库的重新加载，这样我们仅在有新数据时重新加载

blocks.html_en: • or it can be timer based

sentences.en: or it can be timer based
sentences.zh: 或者它可以是基于定时器的

blocks.html_en: • the symfile has to be kept small as we’ll be reloading it a lot.  Although we can be smart about this, and reload it optimistically i.e. use the chksum to quickly see if it has changed, and only reload if required.

sentences.en: the symfile has to be kept small as we’ll be reloading it a lot.
sentences.zh: 符号文件必须保持很小，因为我们会经常重新加载它。

sentences.en: Although we can be smart about this, and reload it optimistically i.e. use the chksum to quickly see if it has changed, and only reload if required.
sentences.zh: 尽管我们可以聪明地处理这一点，并乐观地重新加载它，即使用 chksum 快速查看它是否已更改，并且仅在需要时重新加载。

blocks.html_en: • we cannot have attributes in the “live” partition.  Traditionally hdb partitions put a p attribute on the most commonly filtered column, which acts like an index for lookups.  Attributes on disk in kdb+ are fundamentally not appendable, so we have to do without them.  However we can still sort and add them as normal at rollover as data moves to history and out of the “live partition”

sentences.en: we cannot have attributes in the “live” partition.
sentences.zh: 我们不能在“活动”分区中拥有属性。

sentences.en: Traditionally hdb partitions put a p attribute on the most commonly filtered column, which acts like an index for lookups.
sentences.zh: 传统上，hdb 分区在最常过滤的列上放置 p 属性，其作用类似于查找索引。

sentences.en: Attributes on disk in kdb+ are fundamentally not appendable, so we have to do without them.
sentences.zh: kdb+ 中磁盘上的属性从根本上来说是不可附加的，所以我们必须不用它们。

sentences.en: However we can still sort and add them as normal at rollover as data moves to history and out of the “live partition”
sentences.zh: 然而，当数据移至历史记录并移出“活动分区”时，我们仍然可以在翻转时像平常一样对它们进行排序和添加

blocks.html_en: Based on the requirements of your specific system the architecture above can then be expanded with realtime engines and/or cache processes to calculate analytics (for example VWAPs, bars etc.).  These values can then be published back into the tickerplant and read from the database processes, or accessed directly

sentences.zh: 根据特定系统的要求，可以使用实时引擎和/或缓存进程来扩展上述架构，以计算分析（例如 VWAP、条形图等）。

sentences.en: These values can then be published back into the tickerplant and read from the database processes, or accessed directly
sentences.zh: 然后可以将这些值发布回tickerplant并从数据库进程中读取，或直接访问

blocks.html_en: I’m not one for long conclusions, but hopefully I’ve made you at least consider an alternate default architecture for kdb+. “no rdb” architectures can be simpler, more scalable and nearly as fast.  I’m certainly not saying this is the “correct” way, simply that it’s something we should consider a little more strongly.  If anyone else has thoughts or wants to discuss these ideas further we’re always happy to hear from you!

sentences.zh: 我不会得出长篇结论，但希望我至少让您考虑了 kdb+ 的替代默认架构。

sentences.en: “no rdb” architectures can be simpler, more scalable and nearly as fast.
sentences.zh: “无 RDB”架构可以更简单、更具可扩展性并且几乎同样快。

sentences.zh: 如果其他人有想法或想进一步讨论这些想法，我们总是很高兴收到您的来信！

content_html_zh:
马特·多尔蒂

kdb+ 以不喜欢循环而闻名（没有臭循环）。 作为 kdb+ 开发人员，我们在可以使用的架构方面拥有很大的灵活性，今天我将尝试说服您我们应该从我们的系统中删除其他东西：rdbs。 没有臭rdb！

在 kdb+ 领域，本质上有两种存储数据的方式：内存中和磁盘上。 数据本身是相同的。 这是现在在列式数据空间中非常常见的一个关键见解（特别是 Apache Arrow），因为它消除了将数据从一个地方移动到另一个地方时的序列化需求，并支持内存映射和从磁盘快速读取数据。 kdb+ 支持一系列不同的架构，但标准或默认模型仍然是同时使用两种主要类型的进程：实时数据库（rdb），数据完全位于进程自己的内存空间中；历史数据库（hdb），数据在磁盘上序列化，内存映射到进程内存空间。

我的开场白是故意挑衅的，而且有点开玩笑。 我真的不认为我们根本不应该使用 RDB。 kdb+ 作为一项技术的主要优势在于其灵活性，而 kdb-x 有望增加更多。 我在这里要提出的论点是，rdbs 不应被视为所有 kdb 系统中的默认选项。 它们一直是 kdb 中的核心进程类型之一，但我相信对于许多问题可能不需要它们，您至少应该考虑不使用它们。 这种方法并不新鲜，并且已部署在许多安装中以及 KX 自己的 Insights 产品中的一些组件中，但有关其何时何地适用的详细信息并不总是清楚。

为什么要有rdb？

更快的查询

对内存中数据的查询当然要快得多，不是吗？ 我在相当标准的硬件上用相当标准的数据整理了一个简单的基准测试：

  • 具有 32GB 内存和附加快速 NVMe SSD 存储的 i7i.xlarge ec2 实例
  • 纽约证券交易所 TAQ 交易数据的单个典型（相对较小）日。 大约 5000 万笔交易和 10GB 磁盘空间
  • 比较内存数据（rdb）、磁盘数据（hdb）和缓存冷时磁盘数据（hdb 冷缓存）的查询性能
  • 通过一小组涵盖一些典型工作负载的查询

因此，在我们更多地讨论这些结果之前，关键的背景是 hdb 进程实际上通常不会一直到磁盘来获取数据。 它们将文件内存映射到磁盘上，当访问时，操作系统将首先检查页面缓存，如果该缓存中存在特定文件，它实际上不会去磁盘获取它。 因此，在上面的所有 hdb 结果中，查询都会命中页面缓存而不是磁盘，即缓存是热的。 因此，当缓存处于热状态时，hdb 与 rdb 没有太大区别：它的数据位于内存中，但位于操作系统级页面缓存中，而不是进程自己的内存空间中。 当缓存“冷”时，我还添加了第三组基准测试，因此我们可以看到操作系统必须实际访问磁盘时的差异。

那么这些基准测试的主要结论是什么：

  • 我们不要在这里埋葬lede：内存中是所有基准测试中最快的
  • 如果查询可以使用属性并且结果大小较小，则内存中的数据将大大优于磁盘。 当数据位于内存中并且具有属性时，最后一个查询的速度尤其快。 这些查询实际上是对哈希图的查找，因此 O(1) 并且速度非常快。 我们也可以向磁盘上的数据添加属性，但磁盘上的属性不可在 kdb+ 中附加，因此我们无法维护它们。 我稍后会回到这一点。
  • 如果查询不使用属性，或者使用属性但结果较大，则rdb和hdb之间的性能差距很小。 根据查询的不同，它徘徊在 0-20% 之间。 这种微小的差异可能来自于访问页面缓存时的轻微页面错误和 TLB 压力以及其他技术问题，因此不值得过多担心。 操作系统页面缓存中内存中的数据不如 RDBS 堆中的数据快，但也很接近。 上面的聚合和大扫描（过滤器上有属性）的比较可能是最有趣的：这两个查询都受益于属性，但没有属性的 hdb 仍然不甘落后，因为查询仍然需要访问大量数据，因此属性的好处要少得多。 因此，实际上 hdb 可以被视为另一种类型的内存数据库，内存位于共享空间中：操作系统页面缓存
  • 即使查询必须一直到达磁盘，速度差异仍然不是很大。 我们说的是最坏情况下的两倍。 这就是现代 NVMe SSD 发挥巨大作用的地方。 在旋转磁盘上，这种差异会大得多。

跟上插入内容

除了读取数据之外，我们还必须能够添加新数据。 白天有新的交易流入，我们需要能够添加它们。 这是比较这两种设置的另一个基准：

内存中进程在这里具有更明显的优势。 但它可能没有你想象的那么大。 它肯定比您的存储是慢速旋转磁盘时要小得多。 这里的另一个关键点：插入内存是一个串行过程。 当 RDB 插入新数据时，它无法同时提供查询服务。 在后一种情况下情况并非如此，我们可以插入磁盘上的展开表，同时进程可以对数据进行查询。 计算和内存是分开的。

为什么没有rdb？

更快的查询

我知道上面我展示了 RDB（稍微）更快，但这只是针对一个查询。 如果你有 2 个或 10 个怎么办？ 在 RDB 上，您只能串行运行查询。 它不能同时运行查询。 但是，如果您的数据在磁盘上共享（并通过操作系统页面缓存在内存中），则可以并行。 每个单独的查询可能需要稍长的时间，但您可以在单独的进程上同时运行所有 10 个查询。 在 hdb 上慢 10% 的查询，如果您需要运行两次，则速度会快 45%，因为您可以并行运行，例如两个以 110 毫秒的速度并行运行的查询总共花费 110 毫秒，而两个以 100 毫秒的速度串行运行的查询总共花费 200 毫秒。 如果您想在同一系统上支持不同类型的用例，这也是一个很大的架构优势。 例如研究查询和对延迟更敏感的交易查询，而不会互相妨碍。

默认情况下所有内存都是共享的

我们基本上可以免费进行分片和扩展。 RDB 具有固定的内存成本，如果我们想要更多进程，我们需要更多内存，而新进程启动速度很慢，因为它们需要将所有当前数据读入内存。 当您的数据仅在磁盘和操作系统页面缓存中存储一​​次时，您可以在其上放置 10 个进程，或者为什么不可以放置 100 个呢？ 这些进程几乎可以立即启动。 这从根本上分离了内存和计算

无网关

当然，如果您愿意，您仍然可以拥有网关进程，但您不需要它们将 rdb 和 hdb 数据连接在一起。 可以在一个进程中访问所有数据。 用户可以启动自己的单一流程来访问所有数据，包括实时数据和历史数据。

更简单的架构

当将数据附加到“实时”时，在磁盘上重新加载和映射数据时需要考虑一些细节，但总的来说，您的进程和进程类型都较少。 rdb 和 hdb 以及网关之间不存在协调问题，因此您的系统可能会更简单。

那么让我们多谈谈这个……

替代默认架构

标准 kdb+ 架构之所以如此，是有充分理由的。 如果您所处的世界内存更加昂贵，并且所有非易失性存储都是旋转磁盘，那么将数据库一分为二更有意义。 针对磁盘上展开表的插入将比内存慢得多，可能慢几个数量级，并且任何不命中操作系统页面缓存（冷缓存）的查询都会慢得多，同样可能慢几个数量级。 但这不再是我们生活的世界。

我提出了一个替代的默认架构，看起来更像这样：

tickerplant 进程与以前完全相同，但所有数据都通过“writer”进程（本质上是 TorQ wdb 进程）直接写入磁盘，并且所有数据都通过将数据装载到磁盘上的数据库进程（TorQ hdbs）读取。

让我在这里明确一点，这无论如何都不是一个革命性的想法。 我确信目前正在运行的 kdb+ 系统具有与此类似的架构。 我在这里的论点并不是说这是新的，而是我们应该更认真、更广泛地考虑这种替代方案。

这是一张更清晰的图片，显示了我们如何将磁盘数据分为“活动分区”和所有标准历史数据：

我相信这种方法给我们带来了一些显着的优势：

  • 更简单。 更少的流程更少的问题
  • 更加灵活和可扩展。 计算和内存是分开的。 如果我们需要更多的数据库进程，我们可以简单地添加它们，它们没有直接的内存开销，并且它们会很快旋转（与传统的 RDB 不同）。 我们有效地使用操作系统页面缓存作为“免费”内存管理，这是经过核心战斗测试的操作系统代码，因此非常可靠。
  • 数据位于一处。 如果您需要今天和前几天的数据，则无需针对两个不同的进程运行两个查询并处理连接等。所有数据都位于一个位置

当然，软件工程中的一切都是权衡，这里我们所做的权衡是：

  • 与 RDB 相比，查询性能稍慢。 如上所述，差异比您想象的要小得多，但确实存在差异。
  • 在实时附加的分区中，我们不能有属性。 这意味着对于某些“小型查找”类型的查询，性能差异是巨大的。 然而，这些查询可能不是工作流程的重要部分，或者如果您希望在保持这些类型的查询快速的同时获得上述优势，您可以根据需要添加专用缓存进程，即根据需要添加专用缓存，而不是大型昂贵的通用缓存进程（即rdb）

对于“实时追加”数据库（db，不再需要称它们为 hdb 和 rdb）还有一些关键的技术考虑因素：

  • 我们希望将“活动分区”锁定到操作系统页面缓存中，以确保查询很少/永远不需要访问磁盘。 如果数据被大量使用，这种情况就会自然发生，因为操作系统会尝试将频繁访问的数据保留在缓存中。 不过，我们也可以使用 vmtouch 或 tmpfs (RAMdisk) 来直接控制它。 因此，我们可以保证我们始终获得上面的热缓存基准测试结果，而无需访问磁盘。 这设置起来相对简单。
  • 数据库进程和符号文件（枚举）以及实时附加的列之间存在协调问题。 这并不像看起来那么糟糕。 我相信解决此问题的最佳方法是使用 .Q.MAP 来避免每个查询的 mmap 开销，并使用 \l 重新加载符号文件并定期重新映射最新数据。 原则上，这可以非常快（在我上面的测试设置中，运行 \l 需要 0.018ms），并且我们有一些关于如何执行的选项，具体取决于我们想要在系统中进行的可用性和速度之间的权衡：如果它足够便宜，您可以简单地在任何查询之前运行它，或者您可以让编写器在每次插入时触发所有数据库的重新加载，这样我们只在有新数据时重新加载，或者它可以基于计时器
  • 符号文件必须保持很小，因为我们会经常重新加载它。 尽管我们可以聪明地处理这一点，并乐观地重新加载它，即使用 chksum 快速查看它是否已更改，并且仅在需要时重新加载。
  • 我们不能在“活动”分区中拥有属性。 传统上，hdb 分区在最常过滤的列上放置 p 属性，其作用类似于查找索引。 kdb+ 中磁盘上的属性从根本上来说是不可附加的，所以我们必须不用它们。 然而，当数据移至历史记录并移出“活动分区”时，我们仍然可以在翻转时像平常一样对它们进行排序和添加

根据特定系统的要求，可以使用实时引擎和/或缓存进程来扩展上述架构，以计算分析（例如 VWAP、条形图等）。 然后可以将这些值发布回tickerplant并从数据库进程中读取，或直接访问

  • 滚下
  • 使设置和使用尽可能简单

我不会得出长篇结论，但希望我至少让您考虑了 kdb+ 的替代默认架构。 “无 RDB”架构可以更简单、更具可扩展性并且几乎同样快。 我当然不是说这是“正确”的方式，只是说这是我们应该更强烈地考虑的事情。 如果其他人有想法或想进一步讨论这些想法，我们总是很高兴收到您的来信！
